findings.message: Logging the caller's email address (PII / GDPR personal data) at INFO level is a clear regulatory violation. Application logs typically have broad retention and access, no purpose-limitation, no pseudonymisation, and no documented deletion / right-to-erasure path — so emitting raw emails into them creates personal data with no lawful retention or erasure mechanism.
findings.suggestion: Do not log the raw email. Either drop it entirely, or log a stable pseudonymous identifier (e.g. HMAC/hash of the email with a server-side secret) so the log line is not personal data. If the email truly must be logged for an auditable reason, route it through a dedicated audit log with documented retention, access controls, and an erasure procedure — not the generic application logger.

findings.message: `search_items` is a personalised lookup keyed by a user email (personal data) but has no audit log of WHO accessed WHAT and WHEN (actor, subject email, filter, timestamp, correlation id) — only an INFO line via the app logger, which is not an audit trail. GDPR Art. 30/32 expects auditable records of processing for personalised access to personal data, and there is no retention/erasure path for the email captured here.
findings.suggestion: Treat personalised search as a sensitive operation: write a structured audit-log entry to a dedicated, access-controlled audit sink (separate from app logs) recording actor, hashed/pseudonymised subject identifier, action, timestamp, and correlation id, with a defined retention policy and an erasure hook tied to the subject's right-to-erasure workflow. Also avoid passing identifying email as a query-string parameter (it lands in access logs / proxies) — accept the subject from an authenticated session/token instead.
